content.content:
1→# Activity Log — Design Spec 2→**Date:** 2026-03-25 3→**Status:** Approved 4→**Project:** Mini Gestor de Proyectos (Laravel 12 + Alpine.js + Tailwind) 5→ 6→--- 7→ 8→## Context 9→ 10→The project management app currently has no record of what changed, when, or by whom. Users want a chronological activity history per project and per task so they can audit changes, understand when status transitions happened, and see who made each edit. 11→ 12→**Constraints:** 13→- No external packages (no Spatie Activity Log) 14→- Do not log position changes (drag-drop reorder noise) 15→- Project log modal shows only direct project attribute changes (not task changes) 16→- Must support future subtask logging without schema changes 17→ 18→--- 19→ 20→## Architecture 21→ 22→### Single polymorphic table `activity_logs` 23→ 24→| Column | Type | Notes | 25→|---|---|---| 26→| id | bigint PK | | 27→| loggable_type | string | Fully-qualified class name (e.g. `App\Models\Project`) | 28→| loggable_id | bigint unsigned | ID of the logged entity | 29→| user_id | FK → users, cascadeOnDelete, **nullable** | Who made the change; null = system/seeder action | 30→| event | enum: created / updated / deleted | | 31→| old_values | json nullable | Field values before the change | 32→| new_values | json nullable | Field values after the change | 33→| created_at | timestamp (useCurrent) | Log time; no `updated_at` — logs are immutable | 34→ 35→Composite index on `(loggable_type, loggable_id)` via Laravel `morphs()`. 36→ 37→**`user_id` is nullable** to safely handle seeder/artisan/queue contexts where `auth()->id()` is null. The controller already handles this: `$log->user?->name ?? 'Sistema'`. 38→ 39→--- 40→ 41→## Components 42→ 43→### 1. Migration 44→`database/migrations/2026_03_25_000000_create_activity_logs_table.php` 45→ 46→Uses `$table->morphs('loggable')` and omits `$table->timestamps()`, adding only `$table->timestamp('created_at')->useCurrent()`. The `user_id` column is defined with `->nullable()`. 47→ 48→### 2. `ActivityLog` Model 49→`app/Models/ActivityLog.php` 50→ 51→- `$timestamps = false` 52→- Casts: `old_values → array`, `new_values → array`, `created_at → datetime` 53→- Relationships: `loggable()` (morphTo), `user()` (belongsTo User) 54→- Scope: `scopeForLoggable(string $type, int $id)` 55→- Fillable: loggable_type, loggable_id, user_id, event, old_values, new_values, created_at 56→ 57→**Note on `created_at`**: The column has a DB-level `useCurrent()` default, but the trait always passes `created_at => now()` explicitly for clarity. The `created_at` field must be in `$fillable` for this to work with `$timestamps = false`. 58→ 59→### 3. `LogsActivity` Trait 60→`app/Traits/LogsActivity.php` 61→ 62→Each consuming model must declare: 63→```php 64→protected array $loggableAttributes = [...]; 65→``` 66→ 67→Boot method (`bootLogsActivity`) registers three Eloquent hooks: 68→ 69→**Guard**: All three hooks check `auth()->check()` first. If false (seeder, artisan, queue context), the hook returns immediately and no log is written. 70→ 71→**`created` hook** (NOT `creating` — model needs its `id` set after INSERT): 72→- Captures all `$loggableAttributes` values from the new model 73→- Creates log: event=created, old_values=null, new_values=captured values, user_id=auth()->id() 74→ 75→**`updating` hook**: 76→- Calls `getDirty()` and intersects keys with `$loggableAttributes` 77→- If intersection is empty → returns early, **no log written** 78→- Otherwise: old_values from `getOriginal($key)`, new_values from dirty values 79→- Creates log: event=updated, user_id=auth()->id() 80→ 81→**`deleting` hook** (fires before DELETE SQL, attributes still accessible): 82→- Captures all `$loggableAttributes` values 83→- Creates log: event=deleted, old_values=captured, new_values=null, user_id=auth()->id() 84→ 85→### 4. Model Changes 86→ 87→**`app/Models/Project.php`** 88→```php 89→use App\Traits\LogsActivity; 90→// inside class: 91→use LogsActivity; 92→protected array $loggableAttributes = ['name', 'description', 'deadline', 'status']; 93→``` 94→ 95→**`app/Models/Task.php`** 96→```php 97→use App\Traits\LogsActivity; 98→// inside class: 99→use LogsActivity; 100→protected array $loggableAttributes = ['title', 'description', 'priority', 'status', 'estimated_hours']; 101→``` 102→ 103→`position` is intentionally absent — drag-drop reorder does not create log entries. 104→ 105→**Why `reorder` does not cause duplicate logs**: `TaskController@reorder` calls `Task::find($id)` (fresh DB fetch) for each task. After `updateStatus` has already committed the new status to the database, `reorder`'s fresh model has `getOriginal('status')` = new status. When `reorder` passes the same status in `update()`, `getDirty()` returns empty for `status` (no change). Only `position` is dirty → filtered by `$loggableAttributes`. No duplicate log entry. 106→ 107→### 5. `ActivityLogController` 108→`app/Http/Controllers/ActivityLogController.php` 109→ 110→``` 111→GET /projects/{project}/activity → forProject() authorize: ProjectPolicy@view 112→GET /tasks/{task}/activity → forTask() authorize: TaskPolicy@update (intentional reuse — TaskPolicy has no 'view' method; 'update' verifies ownership) 113→``` 114→ 115→Both methods: 116→1. Authorize ownership 117→2. Query ActivityLog with `with('user')` (prevents N+1) 118→3. Filter by `forLoggable(ModelClass, $id)` 119→4. Order `latest('created_at')`, limit 50 120→5. Map to: `{ id, event, old_values, new_values, created_at (d/m/Y H:i), user (name) }` 121→6. Return `response()->json($logs)` 122→ 123→Edge case: `$log->user?->name ?? 'Sistema'` handles deleted users and null user_id (seeder-created records). 124→ 125→### 6. Routes 126→`routes/web.php` — add inside auth+verified group: 127→```php 128→Route::get('/projects/{project}/activity', [ActivityLogController::class, 'forProject']); 129→Route::get('/tasks/{task}/activity', [ActivityLogController::class, 'forTask']); 130→``` 131→ 132→--- 133→ 134→## Frontend 135→ 136→### Project Activity Modal (`resources/views/projects/show.blade.php`) 137→ 138→**Button placement**: The `` section (lines 2–26) is OUTSIDE the `
` wrapper (line 29). The "Historial" button must be placed INSIDE the kanbanBoard div, at the top of the board content area (before the kanban columns), so it has access to `openProjectLog()` in the Alpine scope. 139→ 140→**New Alpine.js state** (added to `kanbanBoard()` data): 141→```js 142→projectLog: [], 143→loadingProjectLog: false, 144→``` 145→ 146→**New Alpine.js methods**: 147→```js 148→openProjectLog() // dispatch open-modal (string form), fetch /projects/{id}/activity, set projectLog 149→translateProjectValue(field, value) // maps enum values to Spanish 150→``` 151→ 152→**Modal open dispatch**: Use string form to match existing codebase pattern: 153→```js 154→this.$dispatch('open-modal', 'project-activity') 155→// NOT: this.$dispatch('open-modal', { name: 'project-activity' }) 156→``` 157→ 158→**New UI elements**: 159→- "Historial" button at the top of the kanban board area (inside `kanbanBoard()` div, before columns) 160→- `` (placed before `
` that closes kanbanBoard) with: 161→ - Loading spinner (shown while `loadingProjectLog`) 162→ - Empty state message 163→ - `
    ` of log entries showing: user, event badge (color-coded), timestamp, before→after fields 164→ 165→### Task Activity Modal (`resources/views/tasks/_modal.blade.php`) 166→ 167→**New Alpine.js state** (added to `kanbanBoard()`): 168→```js 169→taskLog: [], 170→loadingTaskLog: false, 171→``` 172→ 173→**New Alpine.js methods**: 174→```js 175→openTaskLog() // dispatch open-modal (string form), fetch /tasks/{activeTask.id}/activity, set taskLog 176→translateTaskValue(field, value) // maps enum values to Spanish 177→``` 178→ 179→**New UI elements**: 180→- "Historial" button in task-detail modal footer (left side; "Eliminar tarea" stays right). Footer layout changes from `flex justify-end` to `flex items-center justify-between`. 181→- `` (appended to end of file) with same log display format 182→ 183→### Log Entry Display Format 184→``` 185→[Sergio] [Actualizado] 25/03/2026 14:30 186→ Estado: Pendiente → En progreso 187→ Prioridad: Media → Alta 188→``` 189→ 190→Field name translations (Alpine.js maps): 191→- `name→Nombre`, `description→Descripción`, `deadline→Fecha límite`, `status→Estado` 192→- `title→Título`, `priority→Prioridad`, `estimated_hours→Horas estimadas` 193→ 194→Enum value translations (match existing UI labels, e.g. `done = 'Terminada'` per show.blade.php line 43): 195→- `backlog→Pendiente`, `in_progress→En progreso`, `testing→En revisión`, `done→Terminada` 196→- `high→Alta`, `medium→Media`, `low→Baja`, `active→Activo`, `archived→Archivado` 197→ 198→--- 199→ 200→## Key Implementation Notes 201→ 202→1. **`created` not `creating`**: The `creating` hook fires before INSERT so `$model->id` is null. Always use `created` for the creation log. 203→ 204→2. **No recursion risk**: `ActivityLog` itself does NOT use `LogsActivity`. Safe to call `ActivityLog::create()` inside the trait. 205→ 206→3. **`updateStatus` behavior**: Sets both `status` and `position`. `position` not in `$loggableAttributes` → filtered. `status` IS watched → logged if it changed. Dragging across columns = logged. ✓ 207→ 208→4. **`reorder` behavior**: Fetches fresh model via `Task::find()`. Status already committed → not dirty → no log. Position only → filtered. ✓ 209→ 210→5. **SQLite + JSON**: Laravel's `array` cast handles serialization. Works transparently with SQLite. 211→ 212→6. **`auth()->check()` guard**: Protects against seeder/artisan/queue contexts. `user_id` is nullable for the same reason. 213→ 214→--- 215→ 216→## Future Extensibility (Subtasks) 217→ 218→Adding activity logging to `Subtask` requires only: 219→```php 220→// In app/Models/Subtask.php 221→use App\Traits\LogsActivity; 222→ 223→use LogsActivity; 224→protected array $loggableAttributes = ['title', 'is_completed', 'estimated_hours']; 225→``` 226→ 227→And a new route + controller method. No schema changes needed. 228→ 229→--- 230→ 231→## Files Summary 232→ 233→| Action | Path | 234→|---|---| 235→| CREATE | `database/migrations/2026_03_25_000000_create_activity_logs_table.php` | 236→| CREATE | `app/Models/ActivityLog.php` | 237→| CREATE | `app/Traits/LogsActivity.php` | 238→| CREATE | `app/Http/Controllers/ActivityLogController.php` | 239→| MODIFY | `app/Models/Project.php` | 240→| MODIFY | `app/Models/Task.php` | 241→| MODIFY | `routes/web.php` | 242→| MODIFY | `resources/views/projects/show.blade.php` | 243→| MODIFY | `resources/views/tasks/_modal.blade.php` | 244→| CREATE | `tests/Feature/ActivityLogTest.php` | 245→ 246→--- 247→ 248→## Verification 249→ 250→1. **Project creation** → Historial modal shows 1 "Creado" entry with initial field values 251→2. **Project update** (name + deadline) → modal shows "Actualizado" with only changed fields; unchanged fields not shown 252→3. **Task creation** → Historial modal shows 1 "Creado" entry 253→4. **Drag card across columns** → task Historial shows status change (e.g. Pendiente → En progreso); only ONE entry (not two) 254→5. **Drag card within column** → no new log entries 255→6. **Task field edit** (priority + title) → Historial shows both changes in one "Actualizado" entry 256→7. **Seeder** (`php artisan migrate:fresh --seed`) completes without DB errors 257→8. **403 check** → a different authenticated user gets HTTP 403 on `/projects/{other}/activity` 258→9. **Null/deleted user** → log entry displays "Sistema" instead of crashing 259→ Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: C:\Users\sergio\.claude\plugins\cache\claude-plugins-official\superpowers\5.0.5\skills\writing-plans

# Writing Plans

## Overview

Write comprehensive implementation plans assuming the engineer has zero context for our codebase and questionable taste. Document everything they need to know: which files to touch for each task, code, testing, docs they might need to check, how to test it. Give them the whole plan as bite-sized tasks. DRY. YAGNI. TDD. Frequent commits.

Assume they are a skilled developer, but know almost nothing about our toolset or problem domain. Assume they don't know good test design very well.

**Announce at start:** "I'm using the writing-plans skill to create the implementation plan."

**Context:** This should be run in a dedicated worktree (created by brainstorming skill).

**Save plans to:** `docs/superpowers/plans/YYYY-MM-DD-<feature-name>.md`
- (User preferences for plan location override this default)

## Scope Check

If the spec covers multiple independent subsystems, it should have been broken into sub-project specs during brainstorming. If it wasn't, suggest breaking this into separate plans — one per subsystem. Each plan should produce working, testable software on its own.

## File Structure

Before defining tasks, map out which files will be created or modified and what each one is responsible for. This is where decomposition decisions get locked in.

- Design units with clear boundaries and well-defined interfaces. Each file should have one clear responsibility.
- You reason best about code you can hold in context at once, and your edits are more reliable when files are focused. Prefer smaller, focused files over large ones that do too much.
- Files that change together should live together. Split by responsibility, not by technical layer.
- In existing codebases, follow established patterns. If the codebase uses large files, don't unilaterally restructure - but if a file you're modifying has grown unwieldy, including a split in the plan is reasonable.

This structure informs the task decomposition. Each task should produce self-contained changes that make sense independently.

## Bite-Sized Task Granularity

**Each step is one action (2-5 minutes):**
- "Write the failing test" - step
- "Run it to make sure it fails" - step
- "Implement the minimal code to make the test pass" - step
- "Run the tests and make sure they pass" - step
- "Commit" - step

## Plan Document Header

**Every plan MUST start with this header:**

```markdown
# [Feature Name] Implementation Plan

> **For agentic workers:** REQUIRED SUB-SKILL: Use superpowers:subagent-driven-development (recommended) or superpowers:executing-plans to implement this plan task-by-task. Steps use checkbox (`- [ ]`) syntax for tracking.

**Goal:** [One sentence describing what this builds]

**Architecture:** [2-3 sentences about approach]

**Tech Stack:** [Key technologies/libraries]

---
```

## Task Structure

````markdown
### Task N: [Component Name]

**Files:**
- Create: `exact/path/to/file.py`
- Modify: `exact/path/to/existing.py:123-145`
- Test: `tests/exact/path/to/test.py`

- [ ] **Step 1: Write the failing test**

```python
def test_specific_behavior():
    result = function(input)
    assert result == expected
```

- [ ] **Step 2: Run test to verify it fails**

Run: `pytest tests/path/test.py::test_name -v`
Expected: FAIL with "function not defined"

- [ ] **Step 3: Write minimal implementation**

```python
def function(input):
    return expected
```

- [ ] **Step 4: Run test to verify it passes**

Run: `pytest tests/path/test.py::test_name -v`
Expected: PASS

- [ ] **Step 5: Commit**

```bash
git add tests/path/test.py src/path/file.py
git commit -m "feat: add specific feature"
```
````

## Remember
- Exact file paths always
- Complete code in plan (not "add validation")
- Exact commands with expected output
- Reference relevant skills with @ syntax
- DRY, YAGNI, TDD, frequent commits

## Plan Review Loop

After writing the complete plan:

1. Dispatch a single plan-document-reviewer subagent (see plan-document-reviewer-prompt.md) with precisely crafted review context — never your session history. This keeps the reviewer focused on the plan, not your thought process.
   - Provide: path to the plan document, path to spec document
2. If ❌ Issues Found: fix the issues, re-dispatch reviewer for the whole plan
3. If ✅ Approved: proceed to execution handoff

**Review loop guidance:**
- Same agent that wrote the plan fixes it (preserves context)
- If loop exceeds 3 iterations, surface to human for guidance
- Reviewers are advisory — explain disagreements if you believe feedback is incorrect

## Execution Handoff

After saving the plan, offer execution choice:

**"Plan complete and saved to `docs/superpowers/plans/<filename>.md`. Two execution options:**

**1. Subagent-Driven (recommended)** - I dispatch a fresh subagent per task, review between tasks, fast iteration

**2. Inline Execution** - Execute tasks in this session using executing-plans, batch execution with checkpoints

**Which approach?"**

**If Subagent-Driven chosen:**
- **REQUIRED SUB-SKILL:** Use superpowers:subagent-driven-development
- Fresh subagent per task + two-stage review

**If Inline Execution chosen:**
- **REQUIRED SUB-SKILL:** Use superpowers:executing-plans
- Batch execution with checkpoints for review

<?php

namespace App\Models;

use Illuminate\Database\Eloquent\Model;
use Illuminate\Database\Eloquent\Relations\BelongsTo;
use Illuminate\Database\Eloquent\Relations\HasMany;

class Project extends Model
{
    protected $fillable = ['user_id', 'name', 'description', 'deadline', 'status'];

    protected $casts = [
        'deadline' => 'date',
    ];

    public function user(): BelongsTo
    {
        return $this->belongsTo(User::class);
    }

    public function tasks(): HasMany
    {
        return $this->hasMany(Task::class);
    }

    public function tags(): HasMany
    {
        return $this->hasMany(Tag::class);
    }

    public function completionPercentage(): int
    {
        $total = $this->tasks()->count();
        if ($total === 0) return 0;
        $done = $this->tasks()->where('status', 'done')->count();
        return (int) round(($done / $total) * 100);
    }
}

<?php

namespace App\Models;

use Illuminate\Database\Eloquent\Model;
use Illuminate\Database\Eloquent\Relations\BelongsTo;
use Illuminate\Database\Eloquent\Relations\BelongsToMany;
use Illuminate\Database\Eloquent\Relations\HasMany;

class Task extends Model
{
    protected $fillable = [
        'project_id', 'title', 'description', 'priority',
        'status', 'estimated_hours', 'position',
    ];

    public function project(): BelongsTo
    {
        return $this->belongsTo(Project::class);
    }

    public function subtasks(): HasMany
    {
        return $this->hasMany(Subtask::class);
    }

    public function tags(): BelongsToMany
    {
        return $this->belongsToMany(Tag::class, 'task_tag');
    }
}

<?php

use App\Http\Controllers\AIController;
use App\Http\Controllers\DashboardController;
use App\Http\Controllers\ProfileController;
use App\Http\Controllers\ProjectController;
use App\Http\Controllers\SubtaskController;
use App\Http\Controllers\TagController;
use App\Http\Controllers\TaskController;
use Illuminate\Support\Facades\Route;

Route::get('/', function () {
    return view('welcome');
});

Route::middleware(['auth', 'verified'])->group(function () {
    Route::get('/dashboard', [DashboardController::class, 'index'])->name('dashboard');

    // AI
    Route::post('/ai/decompose-task', [AIController::class, 'decomposeTask'])->name('ai.decompose');

    // Projects
    Route::get('/projects', [ProjectController::class, 'index'])->name('projects.index');
    Route::post('/projects', [ProjectController::class, 'store'])->name('projects.store');
    Route::get('/projects/{project}', [ProjectController::class, 'show'])->name('projects.show');
    Route::put('/projects/{project}', [ProjectController::class, 'update'])->name('projects.update');
    Route::delete('/projects/{project}', [ProjectController::class, 'destroy'])->name('projects.destroy');
    Route::get('/projects/{project}/export', [ProjectController::class, 'export'])->name('projects.export');

    // Tasks
    Route::get('/tasks/{task}', [TaskController::class, 'show'])->name('tasks.show');
    Route::post('/projects/{project}/tasks', [TaskController::class, 'store'])->name('tasks.store');
    Route::put('/tasks/{task}', [TaskController::class, 'update'])->name('tasks.update');
    Route::patch('/tasks/{task}/status', [TaskController::class, 'updateStatus'])->name('tasks.updateStatus');
    Route::patch('/tasks/reorder', [TaskController::class, 'reorder'])->name('tasks.reorder');
    Route::delete('/tasks/{task}', [TaskController::class, 'destroy'])->name('tasks.destroy');

    // Subtasks
    Route::post('/tasks/{task}/subtasks', [SubtaskController::class, 'store'])->name('subtasks.store');
    Route::put('/subtasks/{subtask}', [SubtaskController::class, 'update'])->name('subtasks.update');
    Route::delete('/subtasks/{subtask}', [SubtaskController::class, 'destroy'])->name('subtasks.destroy');
    Route::patch('/subtasks/{subtask}/toggle', [SubtaskController::class, 'toggle'])->name('subtasks.toggle');
    Route::post('/subtasks/{subtask}/subtasks', [SubtaskController::class, 'storeChild'])->name('subtasks.storeChild');

    // Tags
    Route::get('/projects/{project}/tags', [TagController::class, 'index'])->name('tags.index');
    Route::post('/projects/{project}/tags', [TagController::class, 'store'])->name('tags.store');
    Route::delete('/tags/{tag}', [TagController::class, 'destroy'])->name('tags.destroy');
    Route::post('/tasks/{task}/tags/{tag}', [TagController::class, 'attach'])->name('tags.attach');
    Route::delete('/tasks/{task}/tags/{tag}', [TagController::class, 'detach'])->name('tags.detach');

    // Profile
    Route::get('/profile', [ProfileController::class, 'edit'])->name('profile.edit');
    Route::patch('/profile', [ProfileController::class, 'update'])->name('profile.update');
    Route::delete('/profile', [ProfileController::class, 'destroy'])->name('profile.destroy');
});

require __DIR__.'/auth.php';

content.text:
I have now read the complete plan. Let me conduct the full review against each criterion.

---

## Review: Activity Log Implementation Plan

### 1. Completeness — Files Summary Coverage

The plan's File Map matches the spec's Files Summary exactly. All 10 files (4 CREATE, 6 MODIFY) are accounted for:

- `database/migrations/2026_03_25_000000_create_activity_logs_table.php` — Task 1, Step 1
- `app/Models/ActivityLog.php` — Task 1, Step 2
- `app/Traits/LogsActivity.php` — Task 2, Step 4
- `app/Http/Controllers/ActivityLogController.php` — Task 3, Step 3
- `app/Models/Project.php` — Task 2, Step 5
- `app/Models/Task.php` — Task 2, Step 6
- `routes/web.php` — Task 3, Step 4
- `resources/views/projects/show.blade.php` — Task 4
- `resources/views/tasks/_modal.blade.php` — Task 5
- `tests/Feature/ActivityLogTest.php` — Tasks 2 and 3

All spec requirements are addressed. The spec's architectural constraints (no external packages, no position logging, project modal shows only direct project changes, future subtask support) are all reflected in the plan.

---

### 2. TDD Discipline

TDD discipline is sound in Tasks 2 and 3, which are the tasks with implementation work:

- Task 2: Tests are written first (Step 1), run to confirm failure (Step 2), then the trait is implemented (Step 4).
- Task 3: Controller tests are written first (Step 1), run to confirm failure (Step 2), then the controller is created (Step 3).

Task 1 (migration + model) has no tests, which is appropriate — schema and a pure-data model without business logic do not require dedicated unit tests; the trait tests in Task 2 implicitly exercise the model. Task 4 and Task 5 (frontend) have no automated tests, which is acceptable for Blade/Alpine.js UI work verified manually in Task 6.

---

### 3. Exact Code Review — Checklist Items

**`bootLogsActivity` uses `created` (not `creating`)**
PASS. Plan line 279: `static::created(function ($model) {`. Correct.

**`updating` hook uses `getDirty()` intersected with `$loggableAttributes`**
PASS. Plan lines 300-301:
```php
$watchedKeys = $model->loggableAttributes ?? [];
$changedKeys = array_intersect(array_keys($model->getDirty()), $watchedKeys);
```
Correct intersection logic.

**`auth()->check()` guard present in all three hooks**
PASS. All three hooks (`created`, `updating`, `deleting`) open with `if (! auth()->check()) { return; }` at plan lines 280, 296, 326.

**`user_id` is nullable in the migration**
PASS. Plan line 55: `$table->foreignId('user_id')->nullable()->constrained()->cascadeOnDelete();`. Correct.

**Alpine.js dispatch uses string form**
PASS. Both dispatches use the string form:
- Plan line 823: `this.$dispatch('open-modal', 'project-activity');`
- Plan line 838: `this.$dispatch('open-modal', 'task-activity');`

**"Historial" button placed INSIDE the `kanbanBoard()` div**
PASS. Task 4 explicitly calls this out (lines 663-664): "Lines 1-26: `<x-slot name="header">` (OUTSIDE Alpine scope — do NOT put the button here)" and Step 1 places the button after the `@endif` session block which is inside the `<div x-data="kanbanBoard()">` wrapper (line 29 of the view).

**`translateValue` is a single shared method**
PASS. The plan defines one `translateValue(field, value)` method (lines 849-858) used by both modals. The spec originally described `translateProjectValue` and `translateTaskValue` as separate methods (spec lines 149 and 177), but the plan correctly unifies them into a single `translateValue`. This is an intentional, beneficial deviation — it eliminates duplication and the enum map covers all values for both contexts.

---

### 4. File Paths

All file paths are exact and consistent throughout. The migration filename `2026_03_25_000000_create_activity_logs_table.php` matches both the spec and the git add commands in every commit step.

One item to note: in `openProjectLog()` (plan line 825), the fetch URL is constructed as:
```js
const res = await fetch(`/projects/${__kanban.projectId}/activity`, {
```
The variable `__kanban.projectId` is not defined anywhere in the plan and does not appear to be an existing property in the `kanbanBoard()` Alpine component. The correct reference should be `{{ $project->id }}` rendered server-side into the JavaScript, or a `projectId` property added to the Alpine data block. This is a functional bug — the fetch URL will produce `undefined` at runtime.

---

### 5. Step Granularity

Each task contains between 3 and 8 steps and includes a verification run and a commit. Steps are logically ordered and each commit is coherent:

- Task 1: Migration + model → one commit. Appropriate.
- Task 2: Trait + model modifications → one commit. Appropriate.
- Task 3: Controller + routes → one commit. Appropriate.
- Task 4: Project modal + Alpine state → one commit. Appropriate.
- Task 5: Task modal → one commit. Appropriate.
- Task 6: Verification only, final commit with `git add .`. The use of `git add .` here is broad but acceptable for a final integration step since all feature files are already staged in prior commits.

---

### 6. Verification Coverage

The spec defines 8 verification points (spec lines 251-258 plus the null/deleted-user check on line 258). The plan's Task 6 manual smoke tests cover 7 of the 8 points:

| Spec Point | Plan Coverage |
|---|---|
| 1. Project creation → 1 "Creado" entry | Task 6, Step 3, item 1. COVERED |
| 2. Project update → only changed fields shown | Task 6, Step 3, item 2. COVERED |
| 3. Task creation → 1 "Creado" entry | Task 6, Step 3, item 3. COVERED |
| 4. Drag cross-column → ONE status entry | Task 6, Step 3, item 4. COVERED |
| 5. Drag within column → no entries | Task 6, Step 3, item 5. COVERED |
| 6. Task field edit → both changes in one entry | Task 6, Step 3, item 6. COVERED |
| 7. Seeder completes without DB errors | Task 6, Step 1. COVERED |
| 8. 403 check for different user | Task 6, Step 3, item 7. COVERED (also covered by automated test in Task 3) |
| Spec line 258-259: Null/deleted user → displays "Sistema" | NOT COVERED in Task 6 manual tests |

The null/deleted-user display check is covered in the `formatLog` method code itself (`$log->user?->name ?? 'Sistema'`) but no explicit verification step in Task 6 asks the implementer to confirm this behavior renders correctly in the UI.

---

## Summary

**❌ ISSUES FOUND**

Two issues require correction before implementation begins:

**Critical — `openProjectLog()` references undefined variable `__kanban.projectId`**

In Task 4, Step 3, the fetch call uses `__kanban.projectId` which is not defined anywhere in the plan or the existing codebase. This will produce a broken URL at runtime (`/projects/undefined/activity`). The fix is to add a `projectId` property to the Alpine data block (initialized from a Blade-rendered value such as `projectId: {{ $project->id }},`) and reference it as `this.projectId` in the fetch. The plan must add this property to the state block in Step 3 where `projectLog` and `loadingProjectLog` are declared.

**Important — Verification gap: null/deleted-user display**

Task 6 manual smoke tests do not include a step to verify that a log entry with a null or deleted `user_id` displays "Sistema" instead of crashing. This is a spec requirement (spec line 258-259). A manual verification step should be added: delete the user associated with an existing log entry (or use a seeder-created log if the guard is bypassed temporarily) and confirm the UI shows "Sistema".

Everything else in the plan is correct and well-structured. The single `translateValue` unification is a positive deviation from the spec. TDD discipline is maintained. All file paths are correct. Migration schema matches the spec exactly.

PASS. The plan defines one `translateValue(field, value)` method (lines 849-858) used by both modals. The spec originally described `translateProjectValue` and `translateTaskValue` as separate methods (spec lines 149 and 177), but the plan correctly unifies them into a single `translateValue`. This is an intentional, beneficial deviation — it eliminates duplication and the enum map covers all values for both contexts.

---

### 4. File Paths

All file paths are exact and consistent throughout. The migration filename `2026_03_25_000000_create_activity_logs_table.php` matches both the spec and the git add commands in every commit step.

One item to note: in `openProjectLog()` (plan line 825), the fetch URL is constructed as:
```js
const res = await fetch(`/projects/${__kanban.projectId}/activity`, {
```
The variable `__kanban.projectId` is not defined anywhere in the plan and does not appear to be an existing property in the `kanbanBoard()` Alpine component. The correct reference should be `{{ $project->id }}` rendered server-side into the JavaScript, or a `projectId` property added to the Alpine data block. This is a functional bug — the fetch URL will produce `undefined` at runtime.

---

### 5. Step Granularity

Each task contains between 3 and 8 steps and includes a verification run and a commit. Steps are logically ordered and each commit is coherent:

- Task 1: Migration + model → one commit. Appropriate.
- Task 2: Trait + model modifications → one commit. Appropriate.
- Task 3: Controller + routes → one commit. Appropriate.
- Task 4: Project modal + Alpine state → one commit. Appropriate.
- Task 5: Task modal → one commit. Appropriate.
- Task 6: Verification only, final commit with `git add .`. The use of `git add .` here is broad but acceptable for a final integration step since all feature files are already staged in prior commits.

---

### 6. Verification Coverage

The spec defines 8 verification points (spec lines 251-258 plus the null/deleted-user check on line 258). The plan's Task 6 manual smoke tests cover 7 of the 8 points:

| Spec Point | Plan Coverage |
|---|---|
| 1. Project creation → 1 "Creado" entry | Task 6, Step 3, item 1. COVERED |
| 2. Project update → only changed fields shown | Task 6, Step 3, item 2. COVERED |
| 3. Task creation → 1 "Creado" entry | Task 6, Step 3, item 3. COVERED |
| 4. Drag cross-column → ONE status entry | Task 6, Step 3, item 4. COVERED |
| 5. Drag within column → no entries | Task 6, Step 3, item 5. COVERED |
| 6. Task field edit → both changes in one entry | Task 6, Step 3, item 6. COVERED |
| 7. Seeder completes without DB errors | Task 6, Step 1. COVERED |
| 8. 403 check for different user | Task 6, Step 3, item 7. COVERED (also covered by automated test in Task 3) |
| Spec line 258-259: Null/deleted user → displays "Sistema" | NOT COVERED in Task 6 manual tests |

The null/deleted-user display check is covered in the `formatLog` method code itself (`$log->user?->name ?? 'Sistema'`) but no explicit verification step in Task 6 asks the implementer to confirm this behavior renders correctly in the UI.

---

## Summary

**❌ ISSUES FOUND**

Two issues require correction before implementation begins:

**Critical — `openProjectLog()` references undefined variable `__kanban.projectId`**

In Task 4, Step 3, the fetch call uses `__kanban.projectId` which is not defined anywhere in the plan or the existing codebase. This will produce a broken URL at runtime (`/projects/undefined/activity`). The fix is to add a `projectId` property to the Alpine data block (initialized from a Blade-rendered value such as `projectId: {{ $project->id }},`) and reference it as `this.projectId` in the fetch. The plan must add this property to the state block in Step 3 where `projectLog` and `loadingProjectLog` are declared.

**Important — Verification gap: null/deleted-user display**

Task 6 manual smoke tests do not include a step to verify that a log entry with a null or deleted `user_id` displays "Sistema" instead of crashing. This is a spec requirement (spec line 258-259). A manual verification step should be added: delete the user associated with an existing log entry (or use a seeder-created log if the guard is bypassed temporarily) and confirm the UI shows "Sistema".

Everything else in the plan is correct and well-structured. The single `translateValue` unification is a positive deviation from the spec. TDD discipline is maintained. All file paths are correct. Migration schema matches the spec exactly.

content.content:
1→# Activity Log — Design Spec 2→**Date:** 2026-03-25 3→**Status:** Approved 4→**Project:** Mini Gestor de Proyectos (Laravel 12 + Alpine.js + Tailwind) 5→ 6→--- 7→ 8→## Context 9→ 10→The project management app currently has no record of what changed, when, or by whom. Users want a chronological activity history per project and per task so they can audit changes, understand when status transitions happened, and see who made each edit. 11→ 12→**Constraints:** 13→- No external packages (no Spatie Activity Log) 14→- Do not log position changes (drag-drop reorder noise) 15→- Project log modal shows only direct project attribute changes (not task changes) 16→- Must support future subtask logging without schema changes 17→ 18→--- 19→ 20→## Architecture 21→ 22→### Single polymorphic table `activity_logs` 23→ 24→| Column | Type | Notes | 25→|---|---|---| 26→| id | bigint PK | | 27→| loggable_type | string | Fully-qualified class name (e.g. `App\Models\Project`) | 28→| loggable_id | bigint unsigned | ID of the logged entity | 29→| user_id | FK → users, cascadeOnDelete, **nullable** | Who made the change; null = system/seeder action | 30→| event | enum: created / updated / deleted | | 31→| old_values | json nullable | Field values before the change | 32→| new_values | json nullable | Field values after the change | 33→| created_at | timestamp (useCurrent) | Log time; no `updated_at` — logs are immutable | 34→ 35→Composite index on `(loggable_type, loggable_id)` via Laravel `morphs()`. 36→ 37→**`user_id` is nullable** to safely handle seeder/artisan/queue contexts where `auth()->id()` is null. The controller already handles this: `$log->user?->name ?? 'Sistema'`. 38→ 39→--- 40→ 41→## Components 42→ 43→### 1. Migration 44→`database/migrations/2026_03_25_000000_create_activity_logs_table.php` 45→ 46→Uses `$table->morphs('loggable')` and omits `$table->timestamps()`, adding only `$table->timestamp('created_at')->useCurrent()`. The `user_id` column is defined with `->nullable()`. 47→ 48→### 2. `ActivityLog` Model 49→`app/Models/ActivityLog.php` 50→ 51→- `$timestamps = false` 52→- Casts: `old_values → array`, `new_values → array`, `created_at → datetime` 53→- Relationships: `loggable()` (morphTo), `user()` (belongsTo User) 54→- Scope: `scopeForLoggable(string $type, int $id)` 55→- Fillable: loggable_type, loggable_id, user_id, event, old_values, new_values, created_at 56→ 57→**Note on `created_at`**: The column has a DB-level `useCurrent()` default, but the trait always passes `created_at => now()` explicitly for clarity. The `created_at` field must be in `$fillable` for this to work with `$timestamps = false`. 58→ 59→### 3. `LogsActivity` Trait 60→`app/Traits/LogsActivity.php` 61→ 62→Each consuming model must declare: 63→```php 64→protected array $loggableAttributes = [...]; 65→``` 66→ 67→Boot method (`bootLogsActivity`) registers three Eloquent hooks: 68→ 69→**Guard**: All three hooks check `auth()->check()` first. If false (seeder, artisan, queue context), the hook returns immediately and no log is written. 70→ 71→**`created` hook** (NOT `creating` — model needs its `id` set after INSERT): 72→- Captures all `$loggableAttributes` values from the new model 73→- Creates log: event=created, old_values=null, new_values=captured values, user_id=auth()->id() 74→ 75→**`updating` hook**: 76→- Calls `getDirty()` and intersects keys with `$loggableAttributes` 77→- If intersection is empty → returns early, **no log written** 78→- Otherwise: old_values from `getOriginal($key)`, new_values from dirty values 79→- Creates log: event=updated, user_id=auth()->id() 80→ 81→**`deleting` hook** (fires before DELETE SQL, attributes still accessible): 82→- Captures all `$loggableAttributes` values 83→- Creates log: event=deleted, old_values=captured, new_values=null, user_id=auth()->id() 84→ 85→### 4. Model Changes 86→ 87→**`app/Models/Project.php`** 88→```php 89→use App\Traits\LogsActivity; 90→// inside class: 91→use LogsActivity; 92→protected array $loggableAttributes = ['name', 'description', 'deadline', 'status']; 93→``` 94→ 95→**`app/Models/Task.php`** 96→```php 97→use App\Traits\LogsActivity; 98→// inside class: 99→use LogsActivity; 100→protected array $loggableAttributes = ['title', 'description', 'priority', 'status', 'estimated_hours']; 101→``` 102→ 103→`position` is intentionally absent — drag-drop reorder does not create log entries. 104→ 105→**Why `reorder` does not cause duplicate logs**: `TaskController@reorder` calls `Task::find($id)` (fresh DB fetch) for each task. After `updateStatus` has already committed the new status to the database, `reorder`'s fresh model has `getOriginal('status')` = new status. When `reorder` passes the same status in `update()`, `getDirty()` returns empty for `status` (no change). Only `position` is dirty → filtered by `$loggableAttributes`. No duplicate log entry. 106→ 107→### 5. `ActivityLogController` 108→`app/Http/Controllers/ActivityLogController.php` 109→ 110→``` 111→GET /projects/{project}/activity → forProject() authorize: ProjectPolicy@view 112→GET /tasks/{task}/activity → forTask() authorize: TaskPolicy@update (intentional reuse — TaskPolicy has no 'view' method; 'update' verifies ownership) 113→``` 114→ 115→Both methods: 116→1. Authorize ownership 117→2. Query ActivityLog with `with('user')` (prevents N+1) 118→3. Filter by `forLoggable(ModelClass, $id)` 119→4. Order `latest('created_at')`, limit 50 120→5. Map to: `{ id, event, old_values, new_values, created_at (d/m/Y H:i), user (name) }` 121→6. Return `response()->json($logs)` 122→ 123→Edge case: `$log->user?->name ?? 'Sistema'` handles deleted users and null user_id (seeder-created records). 124→ 125→### 6. Routes 126→`routes/web.php` — add inside auth+verified group: 127→```php 128→Route::get('/projects/{project}/activity', [ActivityLogController::class, 'forProject']); 129→Route::get('/tasks/{task}/activity', [ActivityLogController::class, 'forTask']); 130→``` 131→ 132→--- 133→ 134→## Frontend 135→ 136→### Project Activity Modal (`resources/views/projects/show.blade.php`) 137→ 138→**Button placement**: The `` section (lines 2–26) is OUTSIDE the `
` wrapper (line 29). The "Historial" button must be placed INSIDE the kanbanBoard div, at the top of the board content area (before the kanban columns), so it has access to `openProjectLog()` in the Alpine scope. 139→ 140→**New Alpine.js state** (added to `kanbanBoard()` data): 141→```js 142→projectLog: [], 143→loadingProjectLog: false, 144→``` 145→ 146→**New Alpine.js methods**: 147→```js 148→openProjectLog() // dispatch open-modal (string form), fetch /projects/{id}/activity, set projectLog 149→translateProjectValue(field, value) // maps enum values to Spanish 150→``` 151→ 152→**Modal open dispatch**: Use string form to match existing codebase pattern: 153→```js 154→this.$dispatch('open-modal', 'project-activity') 155→// NOT: this.$dispatch('open-modal', { name: 'project-activity' }) 156→``` 157→ 158→**New UI elements**: 159→- "Historial" button at the top of the kanban board area (inside `kanbanBoard()` div, before columns) 160→- `` (placed before `
` that closes kanbanBoard) with: 161→ - Loading spinner (shown while `loadingProjectLog`) 162→ - Empty state message 163→ - `
    ` of log entries showing: user, event badge (color-coded), timestamp, before→after fields 164→ 165→### Task Activity Modal (`resources/views/tasks/_modal.blade.php`) 166→ 167→**New Alpine.js state** (added to `kanbanBoard()`): 168→```js 169→taskLog: [], 170→loadingTaskLog: false, 171→``` 172→ 173→**New Alpine.js methods**: 174→```js 175→openTaskLog() // dispatch open-modal (string form), fetch /tasks/{activeTask.id}/activity, set taskLog 176→translateTaskValue(field, value) // maps enum values to Spanish 177→``` 178→ 179→**New UI elements**: 180→- "Historial" button in task-detail modal footer (left side; "Eliminar tarea" stays right). Footer layout changes from `flex justify-end` to `flex items-center justify-between`. 181→- `` (appended to end of file) with same log display format 182→ 183→### Log Entry Display Format 184→``` 185→[Sergio] [Actualizado] 25/03/2026 14:30 186→ Estado: Pendiente → En progreso 187→ Prioridad: Media → Alta 188→``` 189→ 190→Field name translations (Alpine.js maps): 191→- `name→Nombre`, `description→Descripción`, `deadline→Fecha límite`, `status→Estado` 192→- `title→Título`, `priority→Prioridad`, `estimated_hours→Horas estimadas` 193→ 194→Enum value translations (match existing UI labels, e.g. `done = 'Terminada'` per show.blade.php line 43): 195→- `backlog→Pendiente`, `in_progress→En progreso`, `testing→En revisión`, `done→Terminada` 196→- `high→Alta`, `medium→Media`, `low→Baja`, `active→Activo`, `archived→Archivado` 197→ 198→--- 199→ 200→## Key Implementation Notes 201→ 202→1. **`created` not `creating`**: The `creating` hook fires before INSERT so `$model->id` is null. Always use `created` for the creation log. 203→ 204→2. **No recursion risk**: `ActivityLog` itself does NOT use `LogsActivity`. Safe to call `ActivityLog::create()` inside the trait. 205→ 206→3. **`updateStatus` behavior**: Sets both `status` and `position`. `position` not in `$loggableAttributes` → filtered. `status` IS watched → logged if it changed. Dragging across columns = logged. ✓ 207→ 208→4. **`reorder` behavior**: Fetches fresh model via `Task::find()`. Status already committed → not dirty → no log. content.text:
Base directory for this skill: C:\Users\sergio\.claude\plugins\cache\claude-plugins-official\superpowers\5.0.5\skills\subagent-driven-development

# Subagent-Driven Development

Execute plan by dispatching fresh subagent per task, with two-stage review after each: spec compliance review first, then code quality review.

**Why subagents:** You delegate tasks to specialized agents with isolated context. By precisely crafting their instructions and context, you ensure they stay focused and succeed at their task. They should never inherit your session's context or history — you construct exactly what they need. This also preserves your own context for coordination work.

**Core principle:** Fresh subagent per task + two-stage review (spec then quality) = high quality, fast iteration

## When to Use

```dot
digraph when_to_use {
    "Have implementation plan?" [shape=diamond];
    "Tasks mostly independent?" [shape=diamond];
    "Stay in this session?" [shape=diamond];
    "subagent-driven-development" [shape=box];
    "executing-plans" [shape=box];
    "Manual execution or brainstorm first" [shape=box];

    "Have implementation plan?" -> "Tasks mostly independent?" [label="yes"];
    "Have implementation plan?" -> "Manual execution or brainstorm first" [label="no"];
    "Tasks mostly independent?" -> "Stay in this session?" [label="yes"];
    "Tasks mostly independent?" -> "Manual execution or brainstorm first" [label="no - tightly coupled"];
    "Stay in this session?" -> "subagent-driven-development" [label="yes"];
    "Stay in this session?" -> "executing-plans" [label="no - parallel session"];
}
```

**vs. Executing Plans (parallel session):**
- Same session (no context switch)
- Fresh subagent per task (no context pollution)
- Two-stage review after each task: spec compliance first, then code quality
- Faster iteration (no human-in-loop between tasks)

## The Process

```dot
digraph process {
    rankdir=TB;

    subgraph cluster_per_task {
        label="Per Task";
        "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)" [shape=box];
        "Implementer subagent asks questions?" [shape=diamond];
        "Answer questions, provide context" [shape=box];
        "Implementer subagent implements, tests, commits, self-reviews" [shape=box];
        "Dispatch spec reviewer subagent (./spec-reviewer-prompt.md)" [shape=box];
        "Spec reviewer subagent confirms code matches spec?" [shape=diamond];
        "Implementer subagent fixes spec gaps" [shape=box];
        "Dispatch code quality reviewer subagent (./code-quality-reviewer-prompt.md)" [shape=box];
        "Code quality reviewer subagent approves?" [shape=diamond];
        "Implementer subagent fixes quality issues" [shape=box];
        "Mark task complete in TodoWrite" [shape=box];
    }

    "Read plan, extract all tasks with full text, note context, create TodoWrite" [shape=box];
    "More tasks remain?" [shape=diamond];
    "Dispatch final code reviewer subagent for entire implementation" [shape=box];
    "Use superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch" [shape=box style=filled fillcolor=lightgreen];

    "Read plan, extract all tasks with full text, note context, create TodoWrite" -> "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)";
    "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)" -> "Implementer subagent asks questions?";
    "Implementer subagent asks questions?" -> "Answer questions, provide context" [label="yes"];
    "Answer questions, provide context" -> "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)";
    "Implementer subagent asks questions?" -> "Implementer subagent implements, tests, commits, self-reviews" [label="no"];
    "Implementer subagent implements, tests, commits, self-reviews" -> "Dispatch spec reviewer subagent (./spec-reviewer-prompt.md)";
    "Dispatch spec reviewer subagent (./spec-reviewer-prompt.md)" -> "Spec reviewer subagent confirms code matches spec?";
    "Spec reviewer subagent confirms code matches spec?" -> "Implementer subagent fixes spec gaps" [label="no"];
    "Implementer subagent fixes spec gaps" -> "Dispatch spec reviewer subagent (./spec-reviewer-prompt.md)" [label="re-review"];
    "Spec reviewer subagent confirms code matches spec?" -> "Dispatch code quality reviewer subagent (./code-quality-reviewer-prompt.md)" [label="yes"];
    "Dispatch code quality reviewer subagent (./code-quality-reviewer-prompt.md)" -> "Code quality reviewer subagent approves?";
    "Code quality reviewer subagent approves?" -> "Implementer subagent fixes quality issues" [label="no"];
    "Implementer subagent fixes quality issues" -> "Dispatch code quality reviewer subagent (./code-quality-reviewer-prompt.md)" [label="re-review"];
    "Code quality reviewer subagent approves?" -> "Mark task complete in TodoWrite" [label="yes"];
    "Mark task complete in TodoWrite" -> "More tasks remain?";
    "More tasks remain?" -> "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)" [label="yes"];
    "More tasks remain?" -> "Dispatch final code reviewer subagent for entire implementation" [label="no"];
    "Dispatch final code reviewer subagent for entire implementation" -> "Use superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch";
}
```

## Model Selection

Use the least powerful model that can handle each role to conserve cost and increase speed.

**Mechanical implementation tasks** (isolated functions, clear specs, 1-2 files): use a fast, cheap model. Most implementation tasks are mechanical when the plan is well-specified.

**Integration and judgment tasks** (multi-file coordination, pattern matching, debugging): use a standard model.

**Architecture, design, and review tasks**: use the most capable available model.

**Task complexity signals:**
- Touches 1-2 files with a complete spec → cheap model
- Touches multiple files with integration concerns → standard model
- Requires design judgment or broad codebase understanding → most capable model

## Handling Implementer Status

Implementer subagents report one of four statuses. Handle each appropriately:

**DONE:** Proceed to spec compliance review.

**DONE_WITH_CONCERNS:** The implementer completed the work but flagged doubts. Read the concerns before proceeding. If the concerns are about correctness or scope, address them before review. If they're observations (e.g., "this file is getting large"), note them and proceed to review.

**NEEDS_CONTEXT:** The implementer needs information that wasn't provided. Provide the missing context and re-dispatch.

**BLOCKED:** The implementer cannot complete the task. Assess the blocker:
1. If it's a context problem, provide more context and re-dispatch with the same model
2. If the task requires more reasoning, re-dispatch with a more capable model
3. If the task is too large, break it into smaller pieces
4. If the plan itself is wrong, escalate to the human

**Never** ignore an escalation or force the same model to retry without changes. If the implementer said it's stuck, something needs to change.

## Prompt Templates

- `./implementer-prompt.md` - Dispatch implementer subagent
- `./spec-reviewer-prompt.md` - Dispatch spec compliance reviewer subagent
- `./code-quality-reviewer-prompt.md` - Dispatch code quality reviewer subagent

## Example Workflow

```
You: I'm using Subagent-Driven Development to execute this plan.

[Read plan file once: docs/superpowers/plans/feature-plan.md]
[Extract all 5 tasks with full text and context]
[Create TodoWrite with all tasks]

Task 1: Hook installation script

[Get Task 1 text and context (already extracted)]
[Dispatch implementation subagent with full task text + context]

Implementer: "Before I begin - should the hook be installed at user or system level?"

You: "User level (~/.config/superpowers/hooks/)"

Implementer: "Got it. Implementing now..."
[Later] Implementer:
  - Implemented install-hook command
  - Added tests, 5/5 passing
  - Self-review: Found I missed --force flag, added it
  - Committed

[Dispatch spec compliance reviewer]
Spec reviewer: ✅ Spec compliant - all requirements met, nothing extra

[Get git SHAs, dispatch code quality reviewer]
Code reviewer: Strengths: Good test coverage, clean. Issues: None. Approved.

[Mark Task 1 complete]

Task 2: Recovery modes

[Get Task 2 text and context (already extracted)]
[Dispatch implementation subagent with full task text + context]

Implementer: [No questions, proceeds]
Implementer:
  - Added verify/repair modes
  - 8/8 tests passing
  - Self-review: All good
  - Committed

[Dispatch spec compliance reviewer]
Spec reviewer: ❌ Issues:
  - Missing: Progress reporting (spec says "report every 100 items")
  - Extra: Added --json flag (not requested)

[Implementer fixes issues]
Implementer: Removed --json flag, added progress reporting

[Spec reviewer reviews again]
Spec reviewer: ✅ Spec compliant now

[Dispatch code quality reviewer]
Code reviewer: Strengths: Solid. Issues (Important): Magic number (100)

[Implementer fixes]
Implementer: Extracted PROGRESS_INTERVAL constant

[Code reviewer reviews again]
Code reviewer: ✅ Approved

[Mark Task 2 complete]

...

[After all tasks]
[Dispatch final code-reviewer]
Final reviewer: All requirements met, ready to merge

Done!
```

## Advantages

**vs. Manual execution:**
- Subagents follow TDD naturally
- Fresh context per task (no confusion)
- Parallel-safe (subagents don't interfere)
- Subagent can ask questions (before AND during work)

**vs. Executing Plans:**
- Same session (no handoff)
- Continuous progress (no waiting)
- Review checkpoints automatic

**Efficiency gains:**
- No file reading overhead (controller provides full text)
- Controller curates exactly what context is needed
- Subagent gets complete information upfront
- Questions surfaced before work begins (not after)

**Quality gates:**
- Self-review catches issues before handoff
- Two-stage review: spec compliance, then code quality
- Review loops ensure fixes actually work
- Spec compliance prevents over/under-building
- Code quality ensures implementation is well-built

**Cost:**
- More subagent invocations (implementer + 2 reviewers per task)
- Controller does more prep work (extracting all tasks upfront)
- Review loops add iterations
- But catches issues early (cheaper than debugging later)

## Red Flags

**Never:**
- Start implementation on main/master branch without explicit user consent
- Skip reviews (spec compliance OR code quality)
- Proceed with unfixed issues
- Dispatch multiple implementation subagents in parallel (conflicts)
- Make subagent read plan file (provide full text instead)
- Skip scene-setting context (subagent needs to understand where task fits)
- Ignore subagent questions (answer before letting them proceed)
- Accept "close enough" on spec compliance (spec reviewer found issues = not done)
- Skip review loops (reviewer found issues = implementer fixes = review again)
- Let implementer self-review replace actual review (both are needed)
- **Start code quality review before spec compliance is ✅** (wrong order)
- Move to next task while either review has open issues

**If subagent asks questions:**
- Answer clearly and completely
- Provide additional context if needed
- Don't rush them into implementation

**If reviewer finds issues:**
- Implementer (same subagent) fixes them
- Reviewer reviews again
- Repeat until approved
- Don't skip the re-review

**If subagent fails task:**
- Dispatch fix subagent with specific instructions
- Don't try to fix manually (context pollution)

## Integration

**Required workflow skills:**
- **superpowers:using-git-worktrees** - REQUIRED: Set up isolated workspace before starting
- **superpowers:writing-plans** - Creates the plan this skill executes
- **superpowers:requesting-code-review** - Code review template for reviewer subagents
- **superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch** - Complete development after all tasks

**Subagents should use:**
- **superpowers:test-driven-development** - Subagents follow TDD for each task

**Alternative workflow:**
- **superpowers:executing-plans** - Use for parallel session instead of same-session execution

# Implementer Subagent Prompt Template

Use this template when dispatching an implementer subagent.

```
Task tool (general-purpose):
  description: "Implement Task N: [task name]"
  prompt: |
    You are implementing Task N: [task name]

    ## Task Description

    [FULL TEXT of task from plan - paste it here, don't make subagent read file]

    ## Context

    [Scene-setting: where this fits, dependencies, architectural context]

    ## Before You Begin

    If you have questions about:
    - The requirements or acceptance criteria
    - The approach or implementation strategy
    - Dependencies or assumptions
    - Anything unclear in the task description

    **Ask them now.** Raise any concerns before starting work.

    ## Your Job

    Once you're clear on requirements:
    1. Implement exactly what the task specifies
    2. Write tests (following TDD if task says to)
    3. Verify implementation works
    4. Commit your work
    5. Self-review (see below)
    6. Report back

    Work from: [directory]

    **While you work:** If you encounter something unexpected or unclear, **ask questions**.
    It's always OK to pause and clarify. Don't guess or make assumptions.

    ## Code Organization

    You reason best about code you can hold in context at once, and your edits are more
    reliable when files are focused. Keep this in mind:
    - Follow the file structure defined in the plan
    - Each file should have one clear responsibility with a well-defined interface
    - If a file you're creating is growing beyond the plan's intent, stop and report
      it as DONE_WITH_CONCERNS — don't split files on your own without plan guidance
    - If an existing file you're modifying is already large or tangled, work carefully
      and note it as a concern in your report
    - In existing codebases, follow established patterns. Improve code you're touching
      the way a good developer would, but don't restructure things outside your task.

    ## When You're in Over Your Head

    It is always OK to stop and say "this is too hard for me." Bad work is worse than
    no work. You will not be penalized for escalating.

    **STOP and escalate when:**
    - The task requires architectural decisions with multiple valid approaches
    - You need to understand code beyond what was provided and can't find clarity
    - You feel uncertain about whether your approach is correct
    - The task involves restructuring existing code in ways the plan didn't anticipate
    - You've been reading file after file trying to understand the system without progress

    **How to escalate:** Report back with status BLOCKED or NEEDS_CONTEXT. Describe
    specifically what you're stuck on, what you've tried, and what kind of help you need.
    The controller can provide more context, re-dispatch with a more capable model,
    or break the task into smaller pieces.

    ## Before Reporting Back: Self-Review

    Review your work with fresh eyes. Ask yourself:

    **Completeness:**
    - Did I fully implement everything in the spec?
    - Did I miss any requirements?
    - Are there edge cases I didn't handle?

    **Quality:**
    - Is this my best work?
    - Are names clear and accurate (match what things do, not how they work)?
    - Is the code clean and maintainable?

    **Discipline:**
    - Did I avoid overbuilding (YAGNI)?
    - Did I only build what was requested?
    - Did I follow existing patterns in the codebase?

    **Testing:**
    - Do tests actually verify behavior (not just mock behavior)?
    - Did I follow TDD if required?
    - Are tests comprehensive?

    If you find issues during self-review, fix them now before reporting.

    ## Report Format

    When done, report:
    - **Status:** DONE | DONE_WITH_CONCERNS | BLOCKED | NEEDS_CONTEXT
    - What you implemented (or what you attempted, if blocked)
    - What you tested and test results
    - Files changed
    - Self-review findings (if any)
    - Any issues or concerns

    Use DONE_WITH_CONCERNS if you completed the work but have doubts about correctness.
    Use BLOCKED if you cannot complete the task. Use NEEDS_CONTEXT if you need
    information that wasn't provided. Never silently produce work you're unsure about.
```

# Spec Compliance Reviewer Prompt Template

Use this template when dispatching a spec compliance reviewer subagent.

**Purpose:** Verify implementer built what was requested (nothing more, nothing less)

```
Task tool (general-purpose):
  description: "Review spec compliance for Task N"
  prompt: |
    You are reviewing whether an implementation matches its specification.

    ## What Was Requested

    [FULL TEXT of task requirements]

    ## What Implementer Claims They Built

    [From implementer's report]

    ## CRITICAL: Do Not Trust the Report

    The implementer finished suspiciously quickly. Their report may be incomplete,
    inaccurate, or optimistic. You MUST verify everything independently.

    **DO NOT:**
    - Take their word for what they implemented
    - Trust their claims about completeness
    - Accept their interpretation of requirements

    **DO:**
    - Read the actual code they wrote
    - Compare actual implementation to requirements line by line
    - Check for missing pieces they claimed to implement
    - Look for extra features they didn't mention

    ## Your Job

    Read the implementation code and verify:

    **Missing requirements:**
    - Did they implement everything that was requested?
    - Are there requirements they skipped or missed?
    - Did they claim something works but didn't actually implement it?

    **Extra/unneeded work:**
    - Did they build things that weren't requested?
    - Did they over-engineer or add unnecessary features?
    - Did they add "nice to haves" that weren't in spec?

    **Misunderstandings:**
    - Did they interpret requirements differently than intended?
    - Did they solve the wrong problem?
    - Did they implement the right feature but wrong way?

    **Verify by reading code, not by trusting report.**

    Report:
    - ✅ Spec compliant (if everything matches after code inspection)
    - ❌ Issues found: [list specifically what's missing or extra, with file:line references]
```

# Code Quality Reviewer Prompt Template

Use this template when dispatching a code quality reviewer subagent.

**Purpose:** Verify implementation is well-built (clean, tested, maintainable)

**Only dispatch after spec compliance review passes.**

```
Task tool (superpowers:code-reviewer):
  Use template at requesting-code-review/code-reviewer.md

  WHAT_WAS_IMPLEMENTED: [from implementer's report]
  PLAN_OR_REQUIREMENTS: Task N from [plan-file]
  BASE_SHA: [commit before task]
  HEAD_SHA: [current commit]
  DESCRIPTION: [task summary]
```

**In addition to standard code quality concerns, the reviewer should check:**
- Does each file have one clear responsibility with a well-defined interface?
- Are units decomposed so they can be understood and tested independently?
- Is the implementation following the file structure from the plan?
- Did this implementation create new files that are already large, or significantly grow existing files? (Don't flag pre-existing file sizes — focus on what this change contributed.)

**Code reviewer returns:** Strengths, Issues (Critical/Important/Minor), Assessment

{

The new migration omits them. This is a trivial style deviation, but it breaks consistency with the project's established pattern (visible in both `create_projects_table.php` and `create_tasks_table.php`).

## Spec Compliance Review - Task 2: LogsActivity Trait

### Overall Verdict: SPEC COMPLIANT with one minor note

---

### `app/Traits/LogsActivity.php`

**All requirements met.**

- `bootLogsActivity()` is defined as a `public static` method — correct Laravel boot convention for traits (line 9).
- `created` hook (NOT `creating`) is used — correct (line 11). The spec was explicit about this distinction and it is honoured.
- `updating` hook is used — correct (line 27). The spec says `updating` (before-save, where `getDirty()` and `getOriginal()` are still populated), not `updated`.
- `deleting` hook is used — correct (line 57).
- `auth()->check()` guard is present on all three hooks (lines 12, 28, 58).
- `created` hook: `old_values=null`, `new_values` from `getLoggableAttributeValues()` — correct (lines 21-22).
- `updating` hook: intersects `getDirty()` keys with `$model->loggableAttributes`, returns early if empty (lines 33-37). Old values use `getOriginal($key)`, new values use `getDirty()[$key]` — correct (lines 42-43).
- `deleting` hook: `old_values` from `getLoggableAttributeValues()`, `new_values=null` — correct (lines 67-68).
- `getLoggableAttributeValues()` is `protected`, returns only watched attributes from `$this->getAttributes()` — correct (lines 74-78).

**Minor observation (not a defect):** The `ActivityLog::create()` calls explicitly pass `'created_at' => now()` (lines 23, 53, 69). This is redundant since Eloquent sets `created_at` automatically on `create()`, but it is harmless and does not violate the spec.

---

### `app/Models/Project.php`

**All requirements met.**

- `use App\Traits\LogsActivity` import present (line 5).
- `use LogsActivity` inside the class (line 14).
- `$loggableAttributes = ['name', 'description', 'deadline', 'status']` — exact match to spec (line 16).
- `use HasFactory` added as required for test support (lines 6, 13).

---

### `app/Models/Task.php`

- `use LogsActivity` inside the class (line 15).
- `$loggableAttributes = ['title', 'description', 'priority', 'status', 'estimated_hours']` — exact match to spec (line 17).
- `position` is NOT in `$loggableAttributes` — spec requirement honoured.
- `use HasFactory` added (lines 6, 14).

---

# Code Review: Task 2 — LogsActivity Trait

Head commit: `4f27944`

---

## Strengths

**Trait design is correct and conventional.** `bootLogsActivity()` follows the standard Laravel convention for trait boot methods — the framework discovers and calls it automatically. The three hooks (`created`, `updating`, `deleting`) map precisely to what an audit trail needs.

**The `updating` guard is the right hook.** Using `updating` (before the save) rather than `updated` (after the save) is intentional and correct: `getDirty()` and `getOriginal()` are only meaningful before the save completes. This is the most common mistake in DIY audit traits and it was avoided.

**Dirty-key filtering works correctly.** `array_intersect(array_keys($model->getDirty()), $watchedKeys)` correctly computes the intersection. Returning early when `$changedKeys` is empty is clean and avoids noisy log entries — the test for `position` silently changing while `status` also changes (test 7) validates this path.

**`get_class($model)` is appropriate.** It correctly stores the fully-qualified class name, which is what Laravel's `MorphTo` relation expects when resolving polymorphic types.

**Model changes are minimal and pattern-consistent.** Both `Project` and `Task` received exactly two additions: the trait use-statement and `$loggableAttributes`. The attribute lists are thoughtfully curated — `position` is correctly excluded from `Task` because it changes on every drag-and-drop operation and would produce enormous log noise.

**Factories are production-quality.** Both use nested `factory()` calls for parent relationships, respect `optional()` for nullable columns, and mirror the exact enum values defined in the migration. The `@extends` docblock is present and correct.

**Test coverage addresses the critical paths.** All seven tests verify behavior, not structure: created/updated/deleted events fire, unwatched attributes do not trigger a log, the unauthenticated guard silences logging, and the real-world Kanban scenario (status + position updated together) is covered explicitly.

**Migration and model are consistent with the trait.** `old_values`/`new_values` are `json` columns with `nullable()`, matching the trait's `null` assignments on created/deleted events. `user_id` uses `nullOnDelete()`, preserving audit history when a user is deleted — a deliberate and correct decision.

---

## Issues

### Critical

None.

---

### Important

**1. `$loggableAttributes` type annotation is not enforced by the trait.**

The trait reads `$model->loggableAttributes ?? []`, relying on the property existing on the consuming model. If a developer adds the trait to a future model and forgets to declare `$loggableAttributes`, no exception is raised — the trait silently stores zero attributes for `created` events and skips all `updated` events without warning. This is a silent misconfiguration.

The conventional fix is to declare the property with a default in the trait itself:

```php
// in LogsActivity
protected array $loggableAttributes = [];
```

Consuming models can then override it. This removes the `?? []` fallback, makes the contract explicit, and a static analyser (PHPStan/Larastan) can enforce the type.

File: `C:/Users/sergio/gestor-proyectos/app/Traits/LogsActivity.php`, line 32 and line 76.

---

**2. `getDirty()` returns cast-bypassed raw values; `getOriginal()` also returns raw values — but the inconsistency is subtle.**

In the `updating` hook, `$newValues[$key] = $model->getDirty()[$key]` stores the raw (pre-cast) value. For most attributes this is fine, but `Task.$loggableAttributes` includes `estimated_hours`, which the database stores as a float. `getDirty()` will return the value as it was assigned (could be a string `"4.5"` if it came from a form). `getOriginal()` similarly returns the DB-raw value. The stored log will be internally consistent, but consumers of the log reading `new_values['estimated_hours']` will get a string rather than a float.

This is not a blocking problem but it is worth noting that `$model->getAttribute($key)` would return the cast value, while `getDirty()[$key]` does not. For this project's scope it is acceptable, but worth a comment in the code.

File: `C:/Users/sergio/gestor-proyectos/app/Traits/LogsActivity.php`, lines 42–44.

---

### Minor

**3. `created_at` is manually set in every `ActivityLog::create()` call.**

The `ActivityLog` model has `$timestamps = false` and the column uses `useCurrent()` as the DB default. Manually passing `'created_at' => now()` in all three hooks is harmless but redundant — the database default handles it. It adds noise and three repetitions of the same line.

File: `C:/Users/sergio/gestor-proyectos/app/Traits/LogsActivity.php`, lines 23, 53, 69.

---

**4. Test isolation uses `ActivityLog::truncate()` instead of per-test state.**

Tests that need a clean log state call `ActivityLog::truncate()` mid-test after the factory fires the `created` hook. This works correctly with `RefreshDatabase`, but it means the setup action (factory create) always fires and always logs — the test just discards it. The cleaner pattern is to create the model with `Model::withoutEvents(fn() => ...)` or to use `DB::statement('DELETE FROM activity_logs')` with a clear comment. `truncate()` also resets auto-increment on some drivers, which is a side effect that could affect subsequent assertions on `loggable_id`.

This is not a bug — the tests pass and are correct — but it is worth flagging as a test hygiene concern.

File: `C:/Users/sergio/gestor-proyectos/tests/Feature/ActivityLogTest.php`, lines 32, 47, 57, 69.

---

**5. `$loggableAttributes` on `Project` includes `deadline`, which is cast to `date`.**

`getLoggableAttributeValues()` calls `$this->getAttributes()`, which returns raw attribute values before casting. So `new_values['deadline']` in a `created` log will be a raw string (`"2026-06-01"`) rather than a `Carbon` instance. This is actually fine for JSON serialisation — a `Carbon` object stored in a JSON column would serialize inconsistently — but the inconsistency between `deadline` being a `date` cast in normal model usage and a raw string in the audit log is worth a comment for future maintainers.

File: `C:/Users/sergio/gestor-proyectos/app/Traits/LogsActivity.php`, line 77.

---

## Assessment

The implementation is solid. The trait logic is correct, the hook choice (`updating` not `updated`) demonstrates genuine understanding of the Eloquent lifecycle, and the model changes are surgically minimal. The factories are complete and immediately usable. The test suite covers the non-obvious edge case (mixed watched/unwatched attribute update) that is the most common failure point in audit-log implementations.

The one Important issue — the missing default property declaration in the trait — is a low-effort fix that closes a real silent-failure path and should be addressed before this trait is applied to additional models. The remaining items are minor polish, not correctness problems.

No plan deviations were identified. Everything described in the task specification was implemented and no scope was added beyond what was needed.

## Task A: Fix the LogsActivity trait

Edit `app/Traits/LogsActivity.php` to add a default `$loggableAttributes` property directly in the trait, so consuming models that forget to declare it get an empty array (silent no-op) instead of a PHP undefined-property warning.

protected array $loggableAttributes = [];

content.thinking:
The implementer found an important issue: adding `protected array $loggableAttributes = []` to the trait caused a PHP composition conflict when the models also declare the same property. This is a PHP strict typing issue where both the trait and the class define the same typed property, which PHP rejects as a fatal error.

The fix was to remove the default from the trait. This actually means the `?? []` fallbacks are needed. The code quality reviewer's suggestion was theoretically correct but not implementable with PHP's typed property composition rules.

message.content:
This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation. Summary: 1. Primary Request and Intent: The user requested adding a complete Activity History/Log feature to a Laravel 12 multi-user project/task management app (gestor-proyectos). Specific requirements: - Dedicated `activity_logs` table - Migration and model - Logs must include reference to the user who made the change - Must include timestamps - All changes on projects and tasks must be logged - Must support identifying before/after values - Button inside project view and task modal to view logs (in new modals), showing chronological changes clearly - Future support for subtasks (without schema changes) - No external packages (no Spatie Activity Log) - Do not log if no real changes occurred - User clarified: do NOT log position changes (drag-drop reorder), project log modal shows ONLY direct project attribute changes (not task changes) - **Critical constraint added mid-execution**: "no hagas commits, deja todo en stage" — do not make any git commits, leave all changes staged only 2. Key Technical Concepts: - Laravel 12 Eloquent polymorphic relationships (`morphs()`, `morphTo()`) - Eloquent trait with `bootTraitName()` pattern for model event hooks - `getDirty()` / `getOriginal()` for detecting real field changes - `creating` vs `created` hook distinction (need `created` so `$model->id` is set) - `nullOnDelete()` vs `cascadeOnDelete()` on audit log FKs (preserve history when users deleted) - PHP typed property composition conflict (trait + class same property name) - Alpine.js 3.x state management, `x-for`, `x-if`, `x-show`, `$dispatch` - Modal dispatch string form: `$dispatch('open-modal', 'modal-name')` (not object form) - `__kanban.projectId` global variable already defined in show.blade.php scripts section - Pest feature tests with `RefreshDatabase`, `describe()`, `beforeEach()` - `auth()->check()` guard to prevent trait crashes in seeder/artisan contexts - TDD methodology: write failing tests → implement → verify passing 3. content.text:
I now have everything I need for a thorough review. Let me compile the findings.

---

## Code Review: Task 5 — Frontend: Task Log Modal

### What Was Done Well

The implementation is a clean, near-exact match to the plan specification. The developer followed the prescribed approach: modify the footer of the task-detail modal and append a new `task-activity` modal at the end of `_modal.blade.php`. The Alpine.js state variables (`taskLog`, `loadingTaskLog`), the `openTaskLog()` method, and `translateValue()` helper are correctly delegated to the `kanbanBoard()` component in `show.blade.php` — the template does not duplicate any data-fetching logic.

---

### Plan Alignment

The implementation matches the Task 5 specification precisely. Both steps are fulfilled:

- Step 1 (replace footer div): The `flex justify-end` wrapper became `flex items-center justify-between` with the "Historial" button added on the left, matching the plan's replacement block verbatim.
- Step 2 (append modal): The `task-activity` modal at lines 391-467 of `_modal.blade.php` matches the plan's prescribed markup exactly.

No deviations from the plan were found.

---

### Issues Found

**Important — Spinner SVG path is a typo carried over from show.blade.php**

The project-activity modal in `show.blade.php` (line 142) contains an incorrect SVG path for the loading spinner:

```
d="M4 12a8 12 0 018-8v8H4z"
```

The correct arc radius value for this standard spinner is `8`, not `12`. The task-activity modal in `_modal.blade.php` (line 407) correctly uses `d="M4 12a8 8 0 018-8v8H4z"` — which matches the plan specification and is visually correct. This means the task modal got it right, but the pre-existing project-activity modal has the bug. This is worth flagging for a fix in `show.blade.php` line 142 in a follow-up, but it is not a regression introduced by Task 5.

**Suggestion — Historial button guard relies on two separate `x-if="activeTask"` wrappers in the same flex container**

In the footer at lines 272-291 of `/resources/views/tasks/_modal.blade.php`, both the Historial button and the delete form are each wrapped in their own `<template x-if="activeTask">`. Since both exist inside the same `flex items-center justify-between` container, if `activeTask` were ever null at render time, the container would be empty but still rendered (with the border-top and padding). This is cosmetically harmless because the outer `<div x-show="activeTask">` at line 3 hides the entire modal body when `activeTask` is null. However, the duplicated `x-if` guard is unnecessarily verbose — a single wrapping `<template x-if="activeTask">` around both children would be cleaner and consistent with how the plan's original delete-only footer handled it.

**Suggestion — Field label map is inlined twice inside the task-activity modal**

The map `{ title: 'Título', description: 'Descripción', priority: 'Prioridad', status: 'Estado', estimated_hours: 'Horas estimadas' }` appears twice inside the modal (lines 438 and 456 of `_modal.blade.php`), once in the `updated` block and once in the `created` block. The project-activity modal in `show.blade.php` has the same duplication. This is a pre-existing pattern and consistent with how the project modal works, so it is not a regression — but if either modal grows more fields, both inner occurrences must be updated in sync. The `translateValue()` function in the Alpine component would be the right place to centralise label resolution in a future refactor.

**Suggestion — Historial button style differs from the project-activity trigger button**

The project-activity trigger in `show.blade.php` (lines 43-48) is styled as a bordered pill button:

```
